coverage_notes: Strong coverage of the detraining timeline (Coyle 1984 foundational, Chen 2022 short-term, Zheng 2022 meta-analysis) and fitness retention strategies (Hickson 1981 frequency, Hickson 1985 intensity, Houmard 1990, McConell 1993 volume/intensity, Spiering 2021 minimum dose review). Barbieri 2024 provides comprehensive systematic review of cardiorespiratory/metabolic consequences. Lepers 2024 case study adds retraining data for masters. Neufer 1989 is the classic reduced-training review. Excludes Mujika & Padilla 2000 (both parts), Mujika & Padilla 2001, and Iaia 2023 as directed. Running-specific populations well represented (McConell, Houmard, Chen). Gap: limited data on masters-specific detraining RCTs beyond the Lepers case study.

papers.title: Effects of Short- and Long-Term Detraining on Maximal Oxygen Uptake in Athletes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 21 studies quantifying VO2max decline during short-term vs long-term training cessation across multiple sports.

papers.title: Cardiorespiratory and metabolic consequences of detraining in endurance athletes
papers.notes: Systematic review of cardiorespiratory, metabolic, and muscular detraining consequences in endurance athletes with minimum fitness thresholds.

papers.title: Time course of loss of adaptations after stopping prolonged intense endurance training
papers.notes: Tracked physiological decline at 12, 21, 56, and 84 days after training cessation in endurance-trained subjects, establishing the detraining timeline.

papers.title: Two weeks of detraining reduces cardiopulmonary function and muscular fitness in endurance athletes
papers.notes: Measured cardiopulmonary and muscular fitness changes after 14 days of complete training cessation in young male endurance athletes.

papers.title: Reduced training frequencies and maintenance of increased aerobic power
papers.notes: Demonstrated that VO2max gains from 10 weeks of training were maintained for 15 weeks with only 2-4 sessions per week if intensity was preserved.

papers.title: Reduced training intensities and loss of aerobic power, endurance, and cardiac growth
papers.notes: Showed that reducing training intensity by one-third or two-thirds (with same frequency/duration) caused progressive loss of aerobic power and cardiac adaptations.

papers.title: Maintaining Physical Performance: The Minimal Dose of Exercise Needed to Preserve Endurance and Strength Over Time
papers.journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
papers.notes: Reviewed minimum exercise dose to maintain endurance and strength, concluding intensity is the key variable even with large reductions in frequency and volume.

papers.title: Effect of 12 weeks of detraining and retraining on the cardiorespiratory fitness in a competitive master athlete: a case study
papers.notes: Tracked physiological decline and recovery in a 53-year-old master triathlete through 12 weeks of detraining and 12 weeks of retraining with molecular analysis.
